RESPONDENT.
Written Submissions of the Appellant :- 18.06.2025,24.06.2025
Written Submissions of the Respondent :- 03.09.2024,28.04.2026
Argued on :- 24.02.2026
Judgement on :- 09.07.2026
ANNALINGAM PREMASHANKER, J.
A.
This is an appeal by the Commissioner General of Inland
Revenue (Hereinafter sometimes referred to as The
Respondent/CGIR) from the determination of the Tax Appeals
Commission dated 12.06.2023 made in TAC appeal no.
TAC/IT/048/2021. The Respondent is CEYLON COLD STORES PLC
JUDGEMENT
INTRODUCTION
APPEAL
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 3|14
(Hereinafter sometimes referred to as The Respondent /Tax
Payer)
The Appellant being dissatisfied with the determination of Tax
Appeals Commission (Hereinafter sometimes referred to as TAC)
requested for an appeal by a case stated for the opinion of the
Court of Appeal by their communication dated 11.07.2023.
B.
On 24th February 2026, both parties concluded the argument. The
Appellant has furnished written submissions on 24.06.2025. The
Respondent has furnished the written submissions on 28.04.2026.
C.
There were two questions of law presented for the opinion of the
Court of Appeal, in the case stated dated 27. 07. 2023. They are:
1. Whether the Tax Appeals Commission has erred in law
when determining that the Appellant Company was
denied fair hearing and the Appellant was not given
proper opportunity to present their case before the
Commissioner General of Inland Revenue?
2. Whether the Tax Appeals Commission has erred in law
when determining that is unnecessary to consider the
substantive aspects to this case?
ANALYSIS
ARGUMENT
CASE STATED
QUESTIONS OF LAW
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 4|14
The tax payer submits its return for the year 2016/2017, claiming
an income tax exemption on net income received on security
deposits on bottles and crates. This claim was rejected by the
Assessor and the return is not accepted and issued an
assessment.
Being aggrieved by the said Assessment, the tax payer made an
appeal to the CGIR. The Tax Payer made the same agitation before
the CGIR. CGIR by his determination dated 22.06.2021
determined the appeal by confirming the assessment.
The tax payer being not satisfied with the determination of the
CGIR, made an appeal to the TAC. TAC by its determination dated
12.06.2023, set aside the determination made by the Appellant
and allowed the appeal.
The basic matter in issue is that the Tax Payer is on the business
of selling soft drinks and there is an amount that has been
included as “opening income”, which the Tax Payer states is not
tax liable as this is forfeited deposit by the dealers not returning
the bottles and that there was no disposal of capital assets as the
ownership of the bottles and crates was with the Tax Payer.
TAX RETURN
FIRST APPEAL
SECOND APPEAL
OPENING INCOME
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 5|14
The Appellant states that the Tax Payer recognizes the said
amount as a revenue income. Section 25(3)(a) states that disposal
of a capital asset used in a business is taxed as a revenue income
if the depreciation on cost of acquisition has been claimed. It has
been held and not disputed by the Tax Payer that depreciation has
been claimed. Section 25(7)(b) defines a "capital asset" as any
"equipment" utensil etc used in relation to the trade and business.
The Distributer agreement, clause 5(i) states that the bottles and
crates are given to expand the distributorship and the bottles and
crates are given on deposit and rolled over with each distribution.
The Respondent argues that there is no sale if the bottles and
crates/ capital assets as the ownership is with the Respondent.
However, section 25(7)(d) (ili) describes 'disposal' as
"cessation of the use of such asset". Thus, the "proceeds" that are
taxable is defined in section 25(7)(c) (i) is the amount of the deposit
which is the money recovered, which would be the market value
because no entity would do business other that for profit and the
loss of the bottles and crates needs to be compensated at the
market price.
After hearing the Appeal, the Tax Appeals Commission, by
determination dated 12 June 2023, annulled the assessment
previously confirmed by the Commissioner General of Inland
CAPITAL ASSET
DISPOSAL
CASE STATED
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 6|14
Revenue and allowed the taxpayer’s appeal. Being aggrieved by
the aforesaid determination of the Tax Appeals Commission the
Appellant, Commissioner General of Inland Revenue, made an
application by letter dated 11 July 2023 for a Stated Case, for the
opinion of Court of Appeal on the questions of law enumerated
therein. In the case stated, the Appellant has submit two issues for
the opinion of the Court of Appeal, that is the Tax Payer denied fair
hearing and not given proper opportunity to present their case
before the CGIR and whether the TAC can determine the appeal
without considering the substantial issues.
In the appeal made to the TAC, after hearing the evidence, the
Commission shall on appeal either confirm, reduce, increase or
annul, as the case may be, the assessment as determined by
the Commissioner General or may remit the case to the
Commissioner-General with the decision of the Commission
on such appeal. Where a case is so remitted by the Commission,
the Commissioner General shall revise the assessment in order
that it is in conformity with such amount as stated in the decision
of the Commission. The decision of the Commission shall be
notified to the appellant and the Commissioner-General in writing.
The Appellant asserts that if the TAC was to 'annul' the
assessment, as was done by the TAC in this instant case, they
would have to annul the assessment that was determined by the
Appellant. The TAC never even considered what was determined by
TAC’S POWER
CONSIDERATION
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 7|14
the Appellant CGIR but proceeded to annul the assessments
issued. The word "determined" in this context means, "decided",
"considered" "analyzed". Because the TAC is the final appellate
authority on the facts.
An appeal had been made by the taxpayer. The inquiry was held
on 10 March 2020 by Ms. A I Abeywickrama, Senior Deputy
Commissioner. In the course of such inquiry an agreement was
not reached regarding the matters specified in the petition of
appeal. The Appellant's reasons for the determination dated 12
August 2021 incorrectly indicates that the Respondent had no
objection to referring to the Year of Assessment under review by
the Tax Appeals Commission. This is not the case and the
Respondent required an opportunity for presenting its argument at
a hearing.
The determination of the CGIR is dated 22 June 2021 and it
indicates that no hearing has taken place. This position is
confirmed in the Appellant's reasons for his determination.
It is the Respondent's position that no agreement had been
reached with the Assistant Commissioner as contemplated by
section 165 (7) of the Inland Revenue Act, No 10 of 2006 (as
amended). Therefore, the matter was fixed for hearing by the
Commissioner General of Inland Revenue. Here too no oral
submissions took place at a hearing. This opportunity had been
OPPORTUNITY
PROCEEDING
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 8|14
prevented for no fault made by the taxpayer. The Appellant
afforded no opportunity to the Respondent to present his case at a
hearing.
It is relevant to consider the document marked P3', by which the
Department of Inland Revenue communicated the determination of
the Commissioner General, regarding the Respondent's appeal to
the Commissioner General which had been signed by
Commissioner D.M.S Dissanayake. In communicating the
decision, the writer states that
"...having considered the written and oral submissions made
by authorized representatives and a few officials on behalf of
the appellant company and the views, explanations ruling
submissions made by the Department officials on behalf of
the revenue, I determine the above appeal by confirming the
assessment...”
The Determination, however, gives three dates of hearing, namely
13.12.2012, 21.01.2013 and 07.03.2013 and the notation added
by Commissioner DMS Dissanayake states that
"The above appeals heard before K. Dharmadasa and the
dates of hearing mentioned above are dates of hearing
made by him. "
The above, clearly shows that no hearings had taken place before
Commissioner D. M. S. Dissanayake and for him to say that he
considered 'oral submissions made by authorized representatives
NO HEARING
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 9|14
and a few officials on behalf of the appellant company. ' is patently
incorrect and demands inquiry, not only from the perspective
whether the Respondent was accorded a fair hearing but also
whether the submissions made on behalf of them received due
consideration. Therefore, it is incorrect to state that no prejudice is
allegedly caused to the Respondent.
Further, if the Respondent was not provided with a fair hearing,
then the assessment made by the Commissioner-General is
defective and the fact that the assessment was made by another
assessor is quite apparent. Then the TAC is not required to further
consider the defective assessment of the Commissioner - General,
since the objection of the Respondent goes to the root of the cause.
If the interpretation advanced by the Appellant is accepted, the
Court will inadvertently usurp legislative functions by limiting the
scope of the appeal, when an express limitation is not provided by
the Legislature. In terms of Section 8(1) (a) of the Tax Appeals
Commission Act;
"8(1) Any person aggrieved by the determination of-
a) the Commissioner-General, in respect of any matter
relating to the imposition of any tax, levy, charge, duty or
penalty; or
b) the Director-General under subsection (1B) of section
10 of the Customs Ordinance,
SUBSTANTIVE ISSUES
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 10|14
may if he is dissatisfied with the reasons stated by the
Commissioner-General or the Director- General, as the case may
be, prefer the appeal there from to the Commission within thirty
days from the date of receipt of such reasons; or"
If the Legislature intended the jurisdiction of the TAC to be limited
to only review of the substantive matter of the assessment, why
would the Legislature in Section 8(1)(a) allow an appeal to the TAC
in respect of 'any matter' relating to the imposition of any tax, levy
charge, duty, or penalty of the Commissioner - General? The
rational conclusion that could be drawn is that the Legislature
never intended to limit the jurisdiction of the TAC only to
substantive issues.
In any event, it is the fault of the Appellant to have waited until the
statutory time period for hearing an appeal which was almost at
an end before taking steps to hear and determine the appeal. The
Respondent cannot be faulted for the delay occasioned in hearing
the appeal. Therefore, since there has been a breach of procedural
propriety and the principles of natural justice, the assessment was
annulled and the appeal was allowed.
It has previously been held by the Supreme Court that the Tax
Appeals Commission is entitled to dispose of a matter on a
preliminary objection alone. This has been the basis of the
decision made by the Supreme Court in Seylan Bank PLC v
PROPRIETY
PRELIMINARY ISSUE
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 11|14
Commissioner General of Inland Revenue, SC Appeal No
46/2016. In this case, the decision was made by the Tax Appeals
Commission on a preliminary issue only. Similarly, in
Commissioner General of Inland Revenue v Koggala Garments
(Pvt) Ltd, CA Tax 1/ 2008, the Tax Appeals Commission made its
decision entirely on a preliminary issue.
In Commissioner General of Inland Revenue v Classic Travels
(Pvt) Ltd SC Appeal 55/2024, His Lordship Justice Aluwihare,
PC, J:
The Appellant argued that as per Section 9(10) of the Tax
Appeals Commission Act No.23 of 2011 (as amended) a final
determination cannot be given, which results in the assessment
being annulled if the substantive tax issue is not considered by
the TAC. On this basis the Appellant argues that the TAC
erroneously disposed of its mandate by way of a preliminary
objection. The contention simply stated was that the, the TAC is
only mandated to consider appeals, on merit.
Section 9(10) of the Tax Appeals Commission Act provides that,
"After hearing the evidence, the Commission shall on appeal
either confirm, reduce, increase or annul, as the case may be,
the assessment as determined by the Commissioner- General or
may remit the case to the Commissioner-General with the
decision of the Commission on such appeal. Where a case is so
remitted by the Commission, the Commissioner- General shall
CASE REFERENCE 01
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 12|14
revise the assessment in order that it is in conformity with such
amount as stated in the decision of the Commission.”
I do not find merit in the contention of the Appellant referred to
above, regarding the question of law. The advantage of a
preliminary objection is to prevent unnecessary litigation. Hence,
the purpose of preliminary objections is not to stifle legitimate
adjudication but to dispose a matter expeditiously when it is
apparent that the action cannot be maintained.
The Supreme Court in Commissioner General of Inland
Revenue v Classic Travels (Private) Limited, SC Appeal No
It is a well- established principal that a jurisdictional question
can be decided prior to the substantive issue. Wade and
Forsyth's Administrative Law, states as follows:
Where a jurisdictional question is disputed before a tribunal,
the tribunal must necessarily decide it. If it refuses to do so, it
is wrongfully declining jurisdiction and the court will order it to
act properly. Otherwise the tribunal or other authority 'would be
able to wield an absolutely despotic power, which the
legislature never intended that it should exercise It follows that
the question is within the tribunal's own jurisdiction, but with
this difference, that the tribunal's decision about it cannot be
conclusive. This also was explained in the classic case.
CASE REFERENCE 02
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 13|14
In Commissioner General of Inland Revenue v Classic Travels
(Pvt) Limited, SC Appeal No 158/ 2018, the Supreme Court
judgement of His Lordship Aluwihare J, states as follows:
As judicial review is a collateral challenge and not an appeal,
it will only be in exceptional circumstances that the courts will
allow the collateral process of judicial review to be used to
attack an appealable decision, when Parliament has expressly
provided by statute an appellate procedure. But exceptional
circumstances may arise when it would be unjust for the
taxpayer to appeal a decision of the Commissioner - General.
For example, if the Commissioner - General during the hearing
acted illegally or ultra vires, then exceptional circumstances
may arise where judicial review is appropriate to challenge the
alleged abuse at its inception. Otherwise, the taxpayer would
be expected to proceed with the allegedly illegal hearing until
the determination is issued by the Commissioner - General to
appeal the Commissioner - General's assessment to the TAC.
Accordingly, in my opinion judicial review is not the proper
remedy unless exceptional circumstances justify a claim for
judicial review. If judicial review is eliminated as a remedy
unless exceptional circumstances justify a claim, then the
proper remedy would be an appeal to the TAC, therefore, if
find no merit in the contention of the Appellant.
Judgment CA Tax No. 57/2023 Page 14|14
The above judgements clearly reveal that the TAC can decide the
appeal only on the valid preliminary objection without considering
the substantial issues.
For the reasons adumbrated above, the questions of law are
answered as follows:
Q.N.1. No.
Q.N.2. No.
D.
As analyzed above and as the two questions raised in the case
stated are answered as above, the determination of the TAC
dated 12.06.2023 is affirmed, the appeal is dismissed.
The Registrar is directed to forward a copy of the judgment to the
Tax appeal commission.
On this 9th day of July 2026
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
M. C. B. S. MORAIS
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
CONCLUSION
ANSWERS TO THE QUESTIONS OF LAW
